target.title: fix(ui): scope chat session picker to active agent
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Control UI chat session picker can show sessions from other configured agents because picker search/load-more calls are not scoped by active agent and the popover renders raw `sessions.list` rows.
- Why it matters: multi-agent users can see and accidentally switch into unrelated agent/service sessions from the chat picker.
- What changed: picker requests now include the active `agentId`, and the popover renders rows through the same filtered session-option projection used by the grouped selector.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): the full Sessions page and gateway `sessions.list` behavior remain unchanged.

AI-assisted: Yes. I understand the code path and verified the targeted behavior locally.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #85963
- Supersedes #85964 after rebasing onto current `main`
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: chat session picker pagination/search built `sessions.list` params without the active `agentId`, then rendered `result.sessions` directly instead of using the existing filtered session option groups.
- Missing detection / guardrail: picker tests covered search/pagination mechanics but not cross-agent/cron/subagent filtering in the popover.
- Contributing context (if known): the grouped selector already had active-agent filtering, but the newer popover path bypassed it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when the picker result includes another agent, cron session, and spawned subagent, only active-agent chat sessions render in the popover.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in the Control UI projection/render path, so a focused DOM render test covers the visible regression without a browser E2E fixture.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): grouped selector filtering tests in `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts` covered the helper but not this popover path.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The chat session picker now shows active-agent chat sessions by default instead of leaking unrelated agent/service rows into the popover.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[open chat picker] -> [sessions.list without agentId] -> [raw rows rendered] -> [cross-agent rows visible]

After:
[open chat picker] -> [sessions.list with active agentId] -> [filtered option rows rendered] -> [active-agent sessions visible]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: UI session-list access is narrowed for this picker by passing `agentId` and filtering display rows; broader session management surfaces are unchanged.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local pnpm workspace and installed local OpenClaw Control UI
- Model/provider: N/A for test execution
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat
- Relevant config (redacted): multiple configured agents with agent-scoped session keys

### Steps

1. Open the Control UI chat view with multiple configured agents and sessions.
2. Open the chat session picker.
3. Search/load more from the picker.

### Expected

- Picker requests are scoped to the active agent.
- Popover renders only sessions allowed by the grouped session selector filtering.

### Actual

- Before this PR, picker requests were unscoped and the popover rendered raw `sessions.list` rows.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Redacted runtime diagnostic output
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.10s

pnpm check:test-types
completed successfully

pnpm build
completed successfully
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: the Control UI chat session picker can leak unrelated configured-agent/service rows when it requests sessions without the active agent scope and renders raw response rows.
- Real environment tested: local macOS OpenClaw app, Control UI served from the installed app on `127.0.0.1:18789`, with multiple configured agents present. Private session labels, keys, and structure are intentionally redacted from the proof below.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: refreshed the installed Control UI cache, inspected the served HTML/JS/SW, and queried the live gateway with an explicit active-agent filter. No screenshots are attached because the visible picker contains private session names.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
$ curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/ | rg -n 'index-BtIuF4zW\.js\?v=local-session-picker-fix|local-session-picker-fix'
198:    <script type="module" crossorigin src="./assets/index-BtIuF4zW.js?v=local-session-picker-fix"></script>

$ curl -sS 'http://127.0.0.1:18789/assets/index-BtIuF4zW.js?v=local-session-picker-fix' \
  | rg -o 'agentId:EW\(e,e\.sessionKey\)|i=AW\(e,e\.sessionKey,r\)\.flatMap|local-session-picker-fix'
agentId:EW(e,e.sessionKey)
i=AW(e,e.sessionKey,r).flatMap
local-session-picker-fix

$ curl -sS http://127.0.0.1:18789/sw.js \
  | rg -n 'local-session-picker-fix|cacheName: CACHE_NAME|caches\.match'
5:const EMBEDDED_CACHE_VERSION = "2026.5.22-a374c3a5bfd5-local-session-picker-fix";
64:      caches.match(event.request, { cacheName: CACHE_NAME }).then(
86:        .catch(() => caches.match(event.request)),

$ openclaw gateway call sessions.list --json --params '{"agentId":"main","limit":50}' \
  | jq '{agentScopedCount:(.sessions|length), containsForeignAgents:([.sessions[] | select((.agentId // "main") != "main")] | length > 0), visibleRowsRedacted:[.sessions[:5][] | {key:"<redacted>", label:"<redacted>", agentId:(.agentId // "main"), kind:(.kind // null)}]}'
{
  "agentScopedCount": 22,
  "containsForeignAgents": false,
  "visibleRowsRedacted": [
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "group" },
    { "key": "<redacted>", "label": "<redacted>", "agentId": "main", "kind": "direct" }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the served picker bundle now sends the active `agentId`, projects picker rows through the existing filtered option groups, and the live gateway diagnostic for the active agent reports `containsForeignAgents: false` with session keys/labels redacted.
- What was not tested: public screenshot/recording of the picker, because that would reveal private session names and structure. The runtime diagnostic above is the privacy-preserving proof artifact.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: picker search params include `agentId`; popover filters out other-agent, cron, and spawned subagent rows; targeted UI tests pass; test typecheck passes; full build passes; installed local dist contains the scoped picker request and filtered-row projection.
- Edge cases checked: active session remains visible through existing `resolveSessionOptionGroups` behavior; pagination/search test expectations were updated for the scoped request.
- What you did **not** verify: public browser screenshots in a running Control UI instance, because they would expose private session names and structure.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the picker count can reflect the filtered visible rows while `totalCount` still comes from the server response.
  - Mitigation: server request is now scoped by `agentId`, and existing filtered option logic is reused for display consistency.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(mcp): bound tools/list during catalog discovery
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the original hardening reused `connectionTimeoutMs` as the post-connect MCP `tools/list` request timeout, but that field is documented as connection-only and can hide slow-but-valid catalogs after upgrade.
- Solution: add an explicit optional `toolsListTimeoutMs` for post-connect MCP catalog listing, while preserving the previous SDK/default request-timeout behavior when it is not configured.
- What changed: config type/schema/help/labels/docs now expose `toolsListTimeoutMs`; MCP transport resolution threads it through; catalog discovery passes `{ timeout }` to `client.listTools(...)` only when the new field is set.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no MCP tool execution behavior, auth, transport selection, provider/tool inventory policy, pagination semantics, or default catalog request timeout behavior.

## Motivation

- Related #64411: MCP/plugin failures should degrade predictably instead of blocking the main path.
- Related #82842 covers startup/connect hardening; this PR covers the adjacent post-connect `tools/list` catalog phase without changing the connection-timeout contract.
- Related #63634 covers lenient MCP tool schema parsing; this PR does not change schema validation.
- This is intentionally a narrow compatibility-preserving availability hardening slice; #64411 remains broader follow-up scope.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #: N/A
- Related #64411
- Related #82842
- Related #63634
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: slow-but-valid bundled MCP catalogs remain visible by default even with a low `connectionTimeoutMs`, while an explicitly configured `toolsListTimeoutMs` can bound and degrade a hung post-connect `tools/list` request.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout at `d15d78d91b59919fb4368e2aa5cf7d599d24a28a` on Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64, Node.js v22.22.1.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-slow-list-red-client.mjs
node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-client.mjs
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/mcp.md docs/plugins/bundles.md
node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
$ node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-slow-list-red-client.mjs
{
  "elapsedMs": 558,
  "toolNames": [
    "slow_tool"
  ],
  "serverCount": 1,
  "serverLog": [
    "recv initialize",
    "send 0 response",
    "recv notifications/initialized",
    "delay tools/list 450",
    "recv tools/list",
    "send 1 response"
  ]
}

$ node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-client.mjs
[bundle-mcp] failed to start server "hangingList" (/root/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.1/bin/node /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-server.mjs): McpError: MCP error -32001: Request timed out
{
  "elapsedMs": 505,
  "toolNames": [],
  "serverCount": 0,
  "serverLog": [
    "recv initialize",
    "send 0 response",
    "recv notifications/initialized",
    "recv tools/list",
    "hang tools/list",
    "recv notifications/cancelled"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: without `toolsListTimeoutMs`, the slow 450 ms `tools/list` response produced `slow_tool` even though the fake server config used `connectionTimeoutMs: 250`; with `toolsListTimeoutMs: 250`, the hanging list request was cancelled and catalog discovery resolved degraded/empty in 505 ms.
- What was not tested: live third-party MCP servers, live SSE/streamable HTTP servers, malformed pagination/page-limit behavior, and full repository test suite. HTTP/SSE config threading is covered by resolver tests; source-runtime proof uses stdio.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before the compatibility fix, the same slow-list source-runtime repro with `connectionTimeoutMs: 250` and no `toolsListTimeoutMs` returned `toolNames: []`, `serverCount: 0`, and logged an SDK request timeout before the fake server's delayed `tools/list` response.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the earlier hardening applied the existing `connectionTimeoutMs` to `client.listTools(...)` after connection, broadening a documented connection-only field into a catalog-list request budget.
- Missing detection / guardrail: coverage had a hanging `tools/list` case, but not the compatibility case where a server connects quickly and returns a slow-but-valid catalog under the SDK default request timeout.
- Contributing context (if known): the existing catalog path already catches per-server listing failures and degrades; the missing piece was separating the post-connect list budget from the connection budget.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, `src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: default catalog discovery calls `client.listTools(params)` without a timeout override, an explicit `toolsListTimeoutMs` bounds a hanging list request, and resolver output carries the new optional field for stdio and HTTP transports.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it uses the real session MCP runtime and stdio transport boundary for behavior, plus resolver tests for config threading, while keeping fake servers deterministic and local.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing startup/connect tests covered only connection failure/degrade behavior, not post-connect list compatibility.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; this PR adds regression coverage.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Adds optional `mcp.servers.<name>.toolsListTimeoutMs` for MCP catalog listing. When omitted, OpenClaw preserves the SDK/default request-timeout behavior for `tools/list`; when set, a slow or hung post-connect catalog request is skipped/degraded after that timeout.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Default:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(params) -> SDK/default request timeout remains in effect

Opt-in list timeout:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(params, { timeout: toolsListTimeoutMs }) -> per-server failure is caught -> partial/empty catalog resolves
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No; this only bounds an existing catalog-listing request and does not execute MCP tools.
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: local source checkout, Node.js v22.22.1
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): bundled MCP stdio transport for source-runtime proof; resolver tests cover stdio and HTTP config threading
- Relevant config (redacted): fake slow-list MCP server with `connectionTimeoutMs: 250` and no `toolsListTimeoutMs`; fake hanging MCP server with `connectionTimeoutMs: 30000` and `toolsListTimeoutMs: 250`

### Steps

1. Run the two source-runtime smoke commands shown in `## Real behavior proof`.
2. Run the focused runtime/config regression tests:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
   ```
3. Run related schema/docs/type checks:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
   node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/mcp.md docs/plugins/bundles.md
   node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
   node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
   node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
   ```

### Expected

- A slow-but-valid `tools/list` response remains visible by default when no list timeout is configured.
- A hanging `tools/list` request degrades quickly when `toolsListTimeoutMs` is configured.
- `connectionTimeoutMs` remains a connection-only budget.

### Actual

- Slow-list source-runtime smoke resolved in 544 ms with `toolNames: ["slow_tool"]` and `serverCount: 1`.
- Explicit-timeout source-runtime smoke resolved in 379 ms with `toolNames: []`, `serverCount: 0`, and `notifications/cancelled` in the fake server log.
- Focused MCP/config tests passed: 4 files / 48 tests.
- Config/schema tests passed: 3 files / 76 tests.
- Docs format, generated config schema checks, generated config baseline check, and core test-project typecheck passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: corrected RED behavior before the fix, slow-but-valid default behavior after the fix, explicit list-timeout degradation after the fix, resolver threading for stdio and HTTP, schema/help/docs/generated-baseline alignment, and core test-project typecheck.
- Edge cases checked: non-positive `toolsListTimeoutMs` is ignored like an absent optional timeout, `connectionTimeoutMs` still defaults to 30 seconds when missing, low `connectionTimeoutMs` no longer shortens `tools/list` by default, and fake server cleanup/disposal paths complete.
- What you did **not** verify: live third-party MCP servers, live non-stdio transports, malformed pagination/page-limit behavior, and full repository test suite.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

The existing compatibility finding is addressed by this code update and proof; no manual bot conversation has been resolved in the GitHub UI.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes; this adds optional `mcp.servers.<name>.toolsListTimeoutMs`.
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. Operators who want a shorter post-connect catalog-listing budget can opt in by setting `toolsListTimeoutMs` per MCP server.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a too-low `toolsListTimeoutMs` can still skip a slow-but-valid catalog.
  - Mitigation: the field is opt-in, and the default leaves the SDK/default request timeout in place for compatibility.
- Risk: hung catalogs still wait for the SDK/default request timeout unless the new field is configured.
  - Mitigation: this preserves existing behavior by default and gives operators an explicit list-timeout knob without changing the connection-timeout contract.
- Risk: generated config docs/baseline can drift when adding a new config field.
  - Mitigation: `generate-base-config-schema --check` and `generate-config-doc-baseline --check` passed after regenerating the baseline hash.

target.title: fix(agents/harness): route CLI backend aliases through PI for in-process compaction [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

`selectAgentHarnessDecision` in `src/agents/harness/selection.ts` treated `agentRuntime.id` strictly as a plugin harness id. CLI backend aliases (`claude-cli`, `google-gemini-cli`) are documented-valid runtime ids per the schema (see `runtime-schema.ts`: *"a registered plugin harness id such as 'codex', or a supported CLI backend alias such as 'claude-cli'"*), but they are not plugin harnesses — they are CLI backends dispatched at the request layer by `runCliAgent` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts`.

Normal turns never hit the harness selector for these runtimes because the request layer short-circuits on `isCliProvider` first. But **in-process consumers that call `selectAgentHarness` directly** — most importantly `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, used by both `/compact` and the preflight memory-flush path in `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` — would throw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for any agent whose model-scoped or provider-scoped `agentRuntime.id` was a CLI backend alias.

Once a session crosses the preflight compaction threshold, that throw fires on **every inbound turn across every channel** (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`), because `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` runs before every dispatch. The agent becomes unusable until compacted — and `/compact` itself can't compact, so recovery requires a config swap or surgical patch.

## Fix

Add a `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch to `selectAgentHarnessDecision` right next to the existing `implicit_plugin_unavailable_pi` (codex) fallback. When the runtime is a CLI backend alias, return the PI harness with `runtime: "pi"` stamped on the policy. The new branch is gated by `isCliRuntimeAlias()` from `model-runtime-aliases.ts`, so any future CLI backend added to `LEGACY_RUNTIME_MODEL_PROVIDER_ALIASES` is covered automatically.

This routes only the **in-process step** through PI:
- For compaction: PI does the transcript summarization. Auth still flows through the user's existing CLI OAuth via `resolveClaudeCliSyntheticAuth`.
- For normal turns: unaffected. They never reach this code path because `runAgentAttempt` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts` branch to `runCliAgent` on `isCliProvider` first.

The branch mirrors the existing codex-implicit fallback shape exactly: same `buildSelectionDecision`, same `runtime: "pi"` policy override, same candidate list. No new architecture.

## Why not a CLI-native compaction path?

The CLI backend declares `reseedFromRawTranscriptWhenUncompacted: true` for the claude-cli case (see `cli-backend.ts`), which is the binary's own internal context management — not something the harness layer can call as "summarize this transcript please." Building a CLI-native compaction backend would be a much larger architectural change. PI fallback is the smallest fix consistent with how this function already handles "this runtime can't compact in-process" cases (codex implicit).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: With model-scoped `agentRuntime.id = "claude-cli"`, `selectAgentHarnessDecision` threw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for every call. This blocked `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, so once a session crossed the preflight-compaction threshold, every inbound turn on every channel (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`) failed before reaching the request-layer CLI dispatch. User-visible symptom: an unresponsive bot, with `Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered.` repeating in the gateway log.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS, Apple Silicon, OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed fresh 2 days ago. Single agent (`main`) with `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` as primary model and model-scoped `agentRuntime: { id: "claude-cli" }`. Claude CLI binary at `~/.local/bin/claude`, authenticated via the `anthropic:claude-cli` OAuth profile. Telegram bot channel active. Session at 151% of context (1.6m / 1.0m) when the bug surfaced — preflight compaction firing on every inbound turn.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the semantic equivalent of this PR's patch to the locally installed bundled selector (`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/selection-*.js`). The two-string-literal version of the source change in this PR — added the `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch in `selectAgentHarnessDecision`.
  2. `openclaw gateway restart` — new gateway PID came up, listening on configured port.
  3. Sent a regular message via Telegram to my running bot.
  4. Issued `/compact` in the same Telegram thread.
  5. Tailed `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-$(date +%Y-%m-%d).log` filtered for `MissingAgentHarnessError`, `agent/cli-backend`, and `provider=claude-cli` lines during steps 3–4.

- **Evidence after fix**:
  - **Telegram screenshot** (cropped, redacted) showing the bot's normal reply + `/compact` completing on the same channel that was throwing on every turn before the patch: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8a97397e-22e3-47b9-93e4-4b4d2e353727 (re-attached in my proof comment below for in-thread display).
  - **Before/after gateway log excerpt** (sensitive identifiers redacted, timestamps stripped): https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28105459/proof-logs.txt — attached in my proof comment.
  - Inline excerpt from the before-fix log (sensitive fields scrubbed; 5 of 24 occurrences across `lane=main` and `lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted>`):

    ```
    lane task error: lane=main durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    lane task error: lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted> durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    ```

  - Inline excerpt from the after-fix tail (same scrubbing; representative — every post-restart Telegram turn shows the same `agent/cli-backend` activity):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli model=sonnet promptChars=<redacted> trigger=user useResume=true session=present resumeSession=<redacted> reuse=reusable historyPrompt=present
    claude live session reuse: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6
    claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=<redacted> rawLines=<redacted>
    ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Telegram normal turn replied successfully (the bot answered the prompt I gave it instead of failing the lane).
  - `/compact` returned `Compacted • Context 37k/1.0m (3%)` as a Telegram system message — context dropped from 151% to 3%.
  - Zero `MissingAgentHarnessError` lines in the post-restart filtered tail.
  - Every post-restart Telegram turn logged `provider=claude-cli` from the `agent/cli-backend` subsystem and emitted `claude live session reuse` / `claude live session turn` events. This confirms the request-layer CLI dispatch (`runCliAgent` → `claude` binary subprocess) is unchanged — normal turns still flow through the user's `claude` binary, not the PI fallback added by this patch. The PI fallback is only reached by `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, which is the intended scope.

- **What was not tested**: `google-gemini-cli` runtime end-to-end (no Gemini CLI installation available); covered by unit test in this PR only. Full `pnpm test` matrix beyond the targeted `selection.test.ts` file — happy to run on request. The bundled-JS hot-patch on my local install is semantically equivalent to this PR's source change (uses hardcoded `claude-cli`/`google-gemini-cli` literals because importing into bundled output is impractical; the source PR uses `isCliRuntimeAlias()`).

## Tests added

- `routes CLI backend aliases to the PI harness so in-process compaction works` — verifies `selectAgentHarness` returns the PI harness for both `claude-cli` and `google-gemini-cli` runtimes.
- `lets maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession proceed for claude-cli model runs via PI` — direct regression test for the user-facing bug: the compaction entrypoint resolves without throwing `MissingAgentHarnessError` for a `claude-cli`-aliased anthropic model.

## Related

- Same-shape-of-bug PR: #72499 (helper paths bypassing CLI dispatch — compaction was missed there)
- Issue with the same error message but different root cause (auth migration gap): #84604
- Issue that pointed me at this area: #57326

## AI-assisted disclosure

This patch was authored end-to-end via Claude Code (claude-opus-4-7). The PR author (me) ran the test suite and repo check personally, validated the bundled-JS equivalent of the patch on the live install that originally exhibited the bug (log evidence above), and read every line before pushing. The original bug surfaced on my own machine — the agent's job was to localize the throw site, design the minimum-viable fix consistent with existing patterns (mirroring the codex-implicit fallback), and write the regression tests.

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: fix(cli): avoid false local gateway unreachable on probe timeout
target.body:
## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable
- add regression coverage for the #73535 split: default local probe false-negative while `--require-rpc` or direct `status` RPC succeeds

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.

## Credit
Fixes the non-security path tracked in #48360. Uses reproduction evidence from #62762 and #73535, with historical evidence from #51357 and #42019. Thanks @RacecarGuy, @justinschille, @DJBlackhawk, @tianyaqpzm, and @0xrsydn.

## Validation
- `pnpm check:changed`

ProjectClownfish replacement details:
- Cluster: ghcrawl-156625-autonomous-smoke
- Source PRs: none
- Credit: Preserve reporter credit for #48360 by RacecarGuy, #62762 by justinschille, and #73535 by DJBlackhawk in the PR body.; Mention #51357 by tianyaqpzm and #42019 by 0xrsydn as earlier closed reports that supplied useful historical evidence.; Do not claim or implement #64400's unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation; say that security-shaped sub-scope is routed separately.
- Validation: pnpm check:changed

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(mcp): bound tools/list during catalog discovery
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the original hardening reused `connectionTimeoutMs` as the post-connect MCP `tools/list` request timeout, but that field is documented as connection-only and can hide slow-but-valid catalogs after upgrade.
- Solution: add an explicit optional `toolsListTimeoutMs` for post-connect MCP catalog listing, while preserving the previous SDK/default request-timeout behavior when it is not configured.
- What changed: config type/schema/help/labels/docs now expose `toolsListTimeoutMs`; MCP transport resolution threads it through; catalog discovery passes `{ timeout }` to `client.listTools(...)` only when the new field is set.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no MCP tool execution behavior, auth, transport selection, provider/tool inventory policy, pagination semantics, or default catalog request timeout behavior.

## Motivation

- Related #64411: MCP/plugin failures should degrade predictably instead of blocking the main path.
- Related #82842 covers startup/connect hardening; this PR covers the adjacent post-connect `tools/list` catalog phase without changing the connection-timeout contract.
- Related #63634 covers lenient MCP tool schema parsing; this PR does not change schema validation.
- This is intentionally a narrow compatibility-preserving availability hardening slice; #64411 remains broader follow-up scope.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #: N/A
- Related #64411
- Related #82842
- Related #63634
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: slow-but-valid bundled MCP catalogs remain visible by default even with a low `connectionTimeoutMs`, while an explicitly configured `toolsListTimeoutMs` can bound and degrade a hung post-connect `tools/list` request.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout at `d15d78d91b59919fb4368e2aa5cf7d599d24a28a` on Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64, Node.js v22.22.1.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-slow-list-red-client.mjs
node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-client.mjs
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/mcp.md docs/plugins/bundles.md
node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
$ node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-slow-list-red-client.mjs
{
  "elapsedMs": 558,
  "toolNames": [
    "slow_tool"
  ],
  "serverCount": 1,
  "serverLog": [
    "recv initialize",
    "send 0 response",
    "recv notifications/initialized",
    "delay tools/list 450",
    "recv tools/list",
    "send 1 response"
  ]
}

$ node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-client.mjs
[bundle-mcp] failed to start server "hangingList" (/root/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.1/bin/node /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-server.mjs): McpError: MCP error -32001: Request timed out
{
  "elapsedMs": 505,
  "toolNames": [],
  "serverCount": 0,
  "serverLog": [
    "recv initialize",
    "send 0 response",
    "recv notifications/initialized",
    "recv tools/list",
    "hang tools/list",
    "recv notifications/cancelled"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: without `toolsListTimeoutMs`, the slow 450 ms `tools/list` response produced `slow_tool` even though the fake server config used `connectionTimeoutMs: 250`; with `toolsListTimeoutMs: 250`, the hanging list request was cancelled and catalog discovery resolved degraded/empty in 505 ms.
- What was not tested: live third-party MCP servers, live SSE/streamable HTTP servers, malformed pagination/page-limit behavior, and full repository test suite. HTTP/SSE config threading is covered by resolver tests; source-runtime proof uses stdio.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before the compatibility fix, the same slow-list source-runtime repro with `connectionTimeoutMs: 250` and no `toolsListTimeoutMs` returned `toolNames: []`, `serverCount: 0`, and logged an SDK request timeout before the fake server's delayed `tools/list` response.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the earlier hardening applied the existing `connectionTimeoutMs` to `client.listTools(...)` after connection, broadening a documented connection-only field into a catalog-list request budget.
- Missing detection / guardrail: coverage had a hanging `tools/list` case, but not the compatibility case where a server connects quickly and returns a slow-but-valid catalog under the SDK default request timeout.
- Contributing context (if known): the existing catalog path already catches per-server listing failures and degrades; the missing piece was separating the post-connect list budget from the connection budget.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, `src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: default catalog discovery calls `client.listTools(params)` without a timeout override, an explicit `toolsListTimeoutMs` bounds a hanging list request, and resolver output carries the new optional field for stdio and HTTP transports.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it uses the real session MCP runtime and stdio transport boundary for behavior, plus resolver tests for config threading, while keeping fake servers deterministic and local.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing startup/connect tests covered only connection failure/degrade behavior, not post-connect list compatibility.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; this PR adds regression coverage.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Adds optional `mcp.servers.<name>.toolsListTimeoutMs` for MCP catalog listing. When omitted, OpenClaw preserves the SDK/default request-timeout behavior for `tools/list`; when set, a slow or hung post-connect catalog request is skipped/degraded after that timeout.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Default:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(params) -> SDK/default request timeout remains in effect

Opt-in list timeout:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(params, { timeout: toolsListTimeoutMs }) -> per-server failure is caught -> partial/empty catalog resolves
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No; this only bounds an existing catalog-listing request and does not execute MCP tools.
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: local source checkout, Node.js v22.22.1
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): bundled MCP stdio transport for source-runtime proof; resolver tests cover stdio and HTTP config threading
- Relevant config (redacted): fake slow-list MCP server with `connectionTimeoutMs: 250` and no `toolsListTimeoutMs`; fake hanging MCP server with `connectionTimeoutMs: 30000` and `toolsListTimeoutMs: 250`

### Steps

1. Run the two source-runtime smoke commands shown in `## Real behavior proof`.
2. Run the focused runtime/config regression tests:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
   ```
3. Run related schema/docs/type checks:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
   node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/mcp.md docs/plugins/bundles.md
   node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
   node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
   node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
   ```

### Expected

- A slow-but-valid `tools/list` response remains visible by default when no list timeout is configured.
- A hanging `tools/list` request degrades quickly when `toolsListTimeoutMs` is configured.
- `connectionTimeoutMs` remains a connection-only budget.

### Actual

- Slow-list source-runtime smoke resolved in 544 ms with `toolNames: ["slow_tool"]` and `serverCount: 1`.
- Explicit-timeout source-runtime smoke resolved in 379 ms with `toolNames: []`, `serverCount: 0`, and `notifications/cancelled` in the fake server log.
- Focused MCP/config tests passed: 4 files / 48 tests.
- Config/schema tests passed: 3 files / 76 tests.
- Docs format, generated config schema checks, generated config baseline check, and core test-project typecheck passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: corrected RED behavior before the fix, slow-but-valid default behavior after the fix, explicit list-timeout degradation after the fix, resolver threading for stdio and HTTP, schema/help/docs/generated-baseline alignment, and core test-project typecheck.
- Edge cases checked: non-positive `toolsListTimeoutMs` is ignored like an absent optional timeout, `connectionTimeoutMs` still defaults to 30 seconds when missing, low `connectionTimeoutMs` no longer shortens `tools/list` by default, and fake server cleanup/disposal paths complete.
- What you did **not** verify: live third-party MCP servers, live non-stdio transports, malformed pagination/page-limit behavior, and full repository test suite.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

The existing compatibility finding is addressed by this code update and proof; no manual bot conversation has been resolved in the GitHub UI.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes; this adds optional `mcp.servers.<name>.toolsListTimeoutMs`.
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. Operators who want a shorter post-connect catalog-listing budget can opt in by setting `toolsListTimeoutMs` per MCP server.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a too-low `toolsListTimeoutMs` can still skip a slow-but-valid catalog.
  - Mitigation: the field is opt-in, and the default leaves the SDK/default request timeout in place for compatibility.
- Risk: hung catalogs still wait for the SDK/default request timeout unless the new field is configured.
  - Mitigation: this preserves existing behavior by default and gives operators an explicit list-timeout knob without changing the connection-timeout contract.
- Risk: generated config docs/baseline can drift when adding a new config field.
  - Mitigation: `generate-base-config-schema --check` and `generate-config-doc-baseline --check` passed after regenerating the baseline hash.

target.title: fix(agents/harness): route CLI backend aliases through PI for in-process compaction [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

`selectAgentHarnessDecision` in `src/agents/harness/selection.ts` treated `agentRuntime.id` strictly as a plugin harness id. CLI backend aliases (`claude-cli`, `google-gemini-cli`) are documented-valid runtime ids per the schema (see `runtime-schema.ts`: *"a registered plugin harness id such as 'codex', or a supported CLI backend alias such as 'claude-cli'"*), but they are not plugin harnesses — they are CLI backends dispatched at the request layer by `runCliAgent` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts`.

Normal turns never hit the harness selector for these runtimes because the request layer short-circuits on `isCliProvider` first. But **in-process consumers that call `selectAgentHarness` directly** — most importantly `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, used by both `/compact` and the preflight memory-flush path in `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` — would throw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for any agent whose model-scoped or provider-scoped `agentRuntime.id` was a CLI backend alias.

Once a session crosses the preflight compaction threshold, that throw fires on **every inbound turn across every channel** (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`), because `runPreflightCompactionIfNeeded` runs before every dispatch. The agent becomes unusable until compacted — and `/compact` itself can't compact, so recovery requires a config swap or surgical patch.

## Fix

Add a `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch to `selectAgentHarnessDecision` right next to the existing `implicit_plugin_unavailable_pi` (codex) fallback. When the runtime is a CLI backend alias, return the PI harness with `runtime: "pi"` stamped on the policy. The new branch is gated by `isCliRuntimeAlias()` from `model-runtime-aliases.ts`, so any future CLI backend added to `LEGACY_RUNTIME_MODEL_PROVIDER_ALIASES` is covered automatically.

This routes only the **in-process step** through PI:
- For compaction: PI does the transcript summarization. Auth still flows through the user's existing CLI OAuth via `resolveClaudeCliSyntheticAuth`.
- For normal turns: unaffected. They never reach this code path because `runAgentAttempt` in `attempt-execution.ts` and `agent-runner.runtime.ts` branch to `runCliAgent` on `isCliProvider` first.

The branch mirrors the existing codex-implicit fallback shape exactly: same `buildSelectionDecision`, same `runtime: "pi"` policy override, same candidate list. No new architecture.

## Why not a CLI-native compaction path?

The CLI backend declares `reseedFromRawTranscriptWhenUncompacted: true` for the claude-cli case (see `cli-backend.ts`), which is the binary's own internal context management — not something the harness layer can call as "summarize this transcript please." Building a CLI-native compaction backend would be a much larger architectural change. PI fallback is the smallest fix consistent with how this function already handles "this runtime can't compact in-process" cases (codex implicit).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: With model-scoped `agentRuntime.id = "claude-cli"`, `selectAgentHarnessDecision` threw `MissingAgentHarnessError("claude-cli")` for every call. This blocked `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, so once a session crossed the preflight-compaction threshold, every inbound turn on every channel (Telegram, webchat, SMS, `/compact`) failed before reaching the request-layer CLI dispatch. User-visible symptom: an unresponsive bot, with `Requested agent harness "claude-cli" is not registered.` repeating in the gateway log.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS, Apple Silicon, OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed fresh 2 days ago. Single agent (`main`) with `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` as primary model and model-scoped `agentRuntime: { id: "claude-cli" }`. Claude CLI binary at `~/.local/bin/claude`, authenticated via the `anthropic:claude-cli` OAuth profile. Telegram bot channel active. Session at 151% of context (1.6m / 1.0m) when the bug surfaced — preflight compaction firing on every inbound turn.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the semantic equivalent of this PR's patch to the locally installed bundled selector (`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/selection-*.js`). The two-string-literal version of the source change in this PR — added the `cli_backend_routed_via_pi` branch in `selectAgentHarnessDecision`.
  2. `openclaw gateway restart` — new gateway PID came up, listening on configured port.
  3. Sent a regular message via Telegram to my running bot.
  4. Issued `/compact` in the same Telegram thread.
  5. Tailed `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-$(date +%Y-%m-%d).log` filtered for `MissingAgentHarnessError`, `agent/cli-backend`, and `provider=claude-cli` lines during steps 3–4.

- **Evidence after fix**:
  - **Telegram screenshot** (cropped, redacted) showing the bot's normal reply + `/compact` completing on the same channel that was throwing on every turn before the patch: https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8a97397e-22e3-47b9-93e4-4b4d2e353727 (re-attached in my proof comment below for in-thread display).
  - **Before/after gateway log excerpt** (sensitive identifiers redacted, timestamps stripped): https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28105459/proof-logs.txt — attached in my proof comment.
  - Inline excerpt from the before-fix log (sensitive fields scrubbed; 5 of 24 occurrences across `lane=main` and `lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted>`):

    ```
    lane task error: lane=main durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    lane task error: lane=session:agent:main:telegram:direct:<redacted> durationMs=<redacted> error="MissingAgentHarnessError: Requested agent harness \"claude-cli\" is not registered."
    ```

  - Inline excerpt from the after-fix tail (same scrubbing; representative — every post-restart Telegram turn shows the same `agent/cli-backend` activity):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli model=sonnet promptChars=<redacted> trigger=user useResume=true session=present resumeSession=<redacted> reuse=reusable historyPrompt=present
    claude live session reuse: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6
    claude live session turn: provider=claude-cli model=claude-sonnet-4-6 durationMs=<redacted> rawLines=<redacted>
    ```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Telegram normal turn replied successfully (the bot answered the prompt I gave it instead of failing the lane).
  - `/compact` returned `Compacted • Context 37k/1.0m (3%)` as a Telegram system message — context dropped from 151% to 3%.
  - Zero `MissingAgentHarnessError` lines in the post-restart filtered tail.
  - Every post-restart Telegram turn logged `provider=claude-cli` from the `agent/cli-backend` subsystem and emitted `claude live session reuse` / `claude live session turn` events. This confirms the request-layer CLI dispatch (`runCliAgent` → `claude` binary subprocess) is unchanged — normal turns still flow through the user's `claude` binary, not the PI fallback added by this patch. The PI fallback is only reached by `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, which is the intended scope.

- **What was not tested**: `google-gemini-cli` runtime end-to-end (no Gemini CLI installation available); covered by unit test in this PR only. Full `pnpm test` matrix beyond the targeted `selection.test.ts` file — happy to run on request. The bundled-JS hot-patch on my local install is semantically equivalent to this PR's source change (uses hardcoded `claude-cli`/`google-gemini-cli` literals because importing into bundled output is impractical; the source PR uses `isCliRuntimeAlias()`).

## Tests added

- `routes CLI backend aliases to the PI harness so in-process compaction works` — verifies `selectAgentHarness` returns the PI harness for both `claude-cli` and `google-gemini-cli` runtimes.
- `lets maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession proceed for claude-cli model runs via PI` — direct regression test for the user-facing bug: the compaction entrypoint resolves without throwing `MissingAgentHarnessError` for a `claude-cli`-aliased anthropic model.

## Related

- Same-shape-of-bug PR: #72499 (helper paths bypassing CLI dispatch — compaction was missed there)
- Issue with the same error message but different root cause (auth migration gap): #84604
- Issue that pointed me at this area: #57326

## AI-assisted disclosure

This patch was authored end-to-end via Claude Code (claude-opus-4-7). The PR author (me) ran the test suite and repo check personally, validated the bundled-JS equivalent of the patch on the live install that originally exhibited the bug (log evidence above), and read every line before pushing. The original bug surfaced on my own machine — the agent's job was to localize the throw site, design the minimum-viable fix consistent with existing patterns (mirroring the codex-implicit fallback), and write the regression tests.
